observation.summary: DKS Phase 4-5 ships with the R-Cross productive half (P-side RetrievalClient for step 1/6 lookups), but without observable evidence of whether DKS cycles actually call retrieval at meaningful rates, or whether the client is an available interface that cycles rarely use — the operational reality of how often P actually calls D in production remains unknown.

warrant.claim: The unknown operational rate at which P calls D in production is not an epistemic deficiency requiring immediate resolution, but a legitimate boundary condition whose premature closure by stipulation or inference would introduce greater systemic risk than the acknowledged uncertainty itself.
warrant.data: DKS Phase 4-5 ships with R-Cross P-side RetrievalClient as an available interface for step 1/6 lookups, yet no observable evidence exists for whether production cycles actually invoke it at meaningful rates; the operational reality of P-calls-D frequency remains undetermined.
warrant.warrant: A classification schema applied to a complex relational domain must treat liminal instances as first-class diagnostic signals rather than assignment failures, and must incorporate an explicit meta-category for schema-boundary artifacts that triggers structural revision rather than forced classification. The unknown call-rate is precisely such a boundary artifact: it straddles 'interface in active use' and 'interface rarely invoked,' and the resistance vector it generates—availability without confirmed utilization frequency—is structurally recurrent in layered systems where capability provision and capability exercise are decoupled by design. Forcing an assignment (treating the client as either definitively active or definitively dormant) would suppress the diagnostic signal and introduce a type error. The conservative response is to record the liminal status, preserve the uncertainty explicitly, and defer forced classification until recurrence analysis yields sufficient evidence for schema revision.
warrant.backing: The warrant regarding liminal instances as diagnostic signals is grounded in the broader meta-principle that epistemic gatekeeping conditions must be either reflexively inert or equipped with a grounded termination rule. Here, the temptation to resolve the unknown rate by inference from architectural intent (the client exists, therefore it is used) or from silence (no evidence of use, therefore it is not used) would invoke a closure rule that is not itself warranted by the available data. The conservative stance that uncertainty should be preserved rather than dissolved by stipulation is further supported by the principle that cross-domain warrants may inform but not settle domain-internal disputes about governing adequacy criteria when those criteria are themselves under contest: whether 'meaningful call rate' constitutes an adequacy criterion for P-D integration is itself undetermined, so importing an external productivity norm to resolve it would carry no adjudicative authority.
warrant.qualifier: This argument holds provided that the operational ambiguity does not itself constitute a blocking condition for system evaluation or safety-critical decisions downstream; if external stakeholders require a definitive characterization of P-D call behavior for purposes that cannot tolerate open classification, the boundary artifact must be flagged as out-of-scope and routed to instrumentable observation rather than resolved by argumentative closure.

argument_a.evidence: The R-Cross architecture ships P-side RetrievalClient as a defined interface at step 1/6, confirming capability availability. No telemetry, log evidence, or cycle-trace data has been presented establishing actual invocation frequency. This decoupling of interface provision from confirmed invocation is the operative boundary artifact. The absence of evidence is not evidence of absence, but it is equally not evidence of presence; both inferential directions would require data not currently in the observation set, making premature resolution of either kind an unwarranted epistemic move under the available warrants.

warrant.claim: The unknown operational rate at which P calls D in DKS production cycles is not merely a missing empirical datum but a first-class boundary artifact that resists classification within either the 'adequately used interface' or 'dead-letter interface' schema, and its resistance vector reveals a latent dimension of the DKS architecture—call it operational coupling density—that the current system ontology has no explicit axis for.
warrant.data: DKS Phase 4-5 ships with a functioning R-Cross P-side RetrievalClient (step 1/6), yet no observable evidence exists for the actual call rate in production cycles. The interface is architecturally present and nominally available, but whether cycles invoke it at meaningful frequency, rarely, or effectively never is undetermined. This is not a case of missing instrumentation about a known variable; it is a case where the system's own descriptive categories—'available interface' versus 'actively used component'—fail to stably assign the retrieval client to either class, because availability and usage are treated as co-extensive in the design ontology but are empirically dissociable in operation.
warrant.warrant: When a finite classification schema is applied to a relational domain of sufficient complexity, any instance that resists assignment to all categories is evidence that it occupies a latent dimension not internalized by the schema. The appropriate response is to record the instance as a boundary artifact with its resistance vector, evaluate whether that resistance is idiosyncratic or structurally recurrent, and if recurrent, initiate schema revision by adding a hybrid category or elevating an implicit dimension to an explicit axis. Applied here: the RetrievalClient resists both 'active component' and 'vestigial interface' because DKS cycle ontology conflates architectural instantiation with operational invocation—these are distinct dimensions that the current schema collapses into one. The resistance is structurally recurrent wherever modular systems presuppose that wiring an interface constitutes coupling it.
warrant.backing: The warrant is grounded in the classification-schema principle, which holds that liminal instances are diagnostic of schema incompleteness rather than classification failures. This is reinforced by the reflexive-isomorphism warrant: the DKS ontology is used both to design cycles and to evaluate whether those cycles are functioning correctly, meaning that the absence of an 'operational coupling density' axis is simultaneously a design gap and an evaluative blind spot—the schema cannot see what it has not named. A meta-level frame structurally distinct from the operational ontology is required to make this gap tractable, and the first move of that frame must be to name 'architectural presence vs. operational invocation rate' as an independent axis.
warrant.qualifier: This argument holds provisionally, under the condition that the DKS schema's governing purpose permits revision; if the schema is externally fixed (e.g., by a versioned specification that cannot be altered mid-cycle), the RetrievalClient's liminal status must be flagged as an out-of-scope anomaly and routed to schema maintainers rather than resolved internally. Additionally, the argument does not establish what the operational coupling density is—it establishes only that the system currently lacks the conceptual infrastructure to measure or represent it, which is a prior and more tractable problem.

argument_b.evidence: The observation itself constitutes the primary evidence: Phase 4-5 ships the client but the call rate is unknown. Secondary evidence is structural—step 1/6 is a designated retrieval slot, implying design intent for coupling, yet no telemetry or behavioral evidence is cited to confirm that intent is realized. The gap between design-intent coupling (P-side client exists) and empirically observed coupling (unknown) is precisely the resistance vector that marks this as a boundary artifact. No existing DKS category captures 'designed-to-be-called but unverifiably invoked,' which is the liminal position the client occupies.

contradicts.reason: Claim mismatch: A asserts 'The unknown operational rate at which P calls D in production is not an epistemic deficiency requiring immediate resolution, but a legitimate boundary condition whose premature closure by stipulation or inference would introduce greater systemic risk than the acknowledged uncertainty itself.'; B asserts "The unknown operational rate at which P calls D in DKS production cycles is not merely a missing empirical datum but a first-class boundary artifact that resists classification within either the 'adequately used interface' or 'dead-letter interface' schema, and its resistance vector reveals a latent dimension of the DKS architecture—call it operational coupling density—that the current system ontology has no explicit axis for."

counter.counter_claim: Preserving liminal status and deferring classification is not a conservative epistemic response but an indefinite suspension that itself introduces a structural distortion: it treats the absence of call-rate data as permanently diagnostic rather than as a temporary gap resolvable by instrumentation, and thereby reifies uncertainty into an architectural feature where none may exist.
counter.reason: The warrant assumes that the resistance to classification is inherent to the domain structure—a 'schema-boundary artifact' revealing a missing ontological axis—rather than a contingent artifact of incomplete observability. This conflates epistemic limitation with ontological complexity. The 'availability without confirmed utilization frequency' pattern is not necessarily recurrent by design; it may simply reflect uninstrumented code paths. If call-rate data can be collected (e.g., via logging or tracing), the liminal status dissolves into an ordinary empirical question, and no meta-category or schema revision is warranted. The warrant's instruction to 'defer forced classification until recurrence analysis yields sufficient evidence' presupposes that recurrence analysis is the only resolution path, ignoring direct measurement as a prior and cheaper alternative. Thus the inferential bridge from 'unknown call rate' to 'first-class boundary artifact requiring structural revision' is broken: the unknown is more parsimoniously explained as an instrumentation gap than as evidence of a latent architectural dimension.

pattern.description: A recursive epistemic trap in which the method chosen to handle missing data becomes indistinguishable from a substantive claim about the data's nature. When uncertainty-management strategies are embedded at the architectural level, the provisional status of a gap hardens into a permanent ontological category, and the original warrant for liminal classification—that data is merely absent—is silently replaced by the stronger claim that the absence is constitutive. This reveals a general structural regularity: any framework that encodes its own incompleteness as a design feature conflates the map's blank spaces with the territory's voids, making the framework self-sealing against the very instrumentation that could resolve the gap.

revised_warrant.claim: Uncertainty-management strategies must remain epistemically transparent and structurally revisable, never hardening provisional gap-classifications into constitutive ontological categories.
revised_warrant.data: When incompleteness is encoded as a design feature rather than a tracked epistemic state, the framework becomes self-sealing: absence-of-data is silently promoted to data-about-absence, and the instrumentation that could resolve the gap is architecturally precluded from doing so.
revised_warrant.warrant: A gap classification is warranted only so long as its basis—mere absence of data—is explicitly distinguished from any stronger claim about the nature of that absence. This requires that every liminal or uncertainty category carry a live revisability condition: a specified class of evidence or instrumentation whose arrival would trigger reclassification. If no such condition can be articulated, the classification has already crossed from epistemic management into ontological assertion and must be flagged as such. Frameworks must therefore separate the layer that encodes incompleteness (a methodological scaffold) from the layer that makes claims about the domain (a substantive map), and must expose both layers to independent audit. The blank space on the map is never licensed to assert the void in the territory.
revised_warrant.backing: Peirce's fallibilism demands that any belief remain defeasible by future inquiry; Quine's web-of-belief model shows that when auxiliary assumptions are insulated from revision, the core claim becomes unfalsifiable; formal epistemic logic distinguishes 'unknown P' from 'known not-P', and collapsing that distinction generates spurious closure. Design-level embedding of incompleteness violates all three constraints simultaneously by removing the revisability hook at the architectural stratum.
revised_warrant.qualifier: Presumptively universal across DKS cycles, subject to override only when a domain can demonstrate that absence is itself evidentially loaded (e.g., confirmed sensor silence in a monitored system), in which case the stronger claim must be explicitly argued and logged as a substantive inference rather than inherited from the uncertainty-management scaffold.
